news_summary: Innovative recycling, large-scale reforestation, unique ecological restoration, and resilient tribal clean energy initiatives are driving significant environmental progress today.
news_summary_fr: Le recyclage innovant, le reboisement à grande échelle, la restauration écologique unique et les initiatives tribales résilientes en matière d'énergie propre sont aujourd'hui les moteurs d'importants progrès environnementaux.
news_summary_es: El reciclaje innovador, la reforestación a gran escala, la restauración ecológica única y las iniciativas tribales resilientes de energía limpia están impulsando un progreso medioambiental significativo en la actualidad.

articles.title: Scientists turn scrap car aluminum into high-performance metal for new vehicles
articles.summary: Scientists at Oak Ridge National Laboratory have created a new aluminum alloy called RidgeAlloy that can turn contaminated car-body scrap into strong structural vehicle parts. Normally, impurities introduced during recycling make this scrap unsuitable for high-performance applications. RidgeAlloy overcomes that challenge, enabling recycled aluminum to meet the strength and durability standards required for modern vehicles. The technology could slash energy use, reduce imports, and unlock a huge new supply of domestic aluminum.
articles.content:
Over the next decade, a large amount of aluminum from vehicle body panels is expected to enter recycling and salvage systems. Much of this material cannot currently be reused in critical automotive components because contamination makes it too impure. That limitation has reduced its value.

Researchers at the Department of Energy's (DOE) Oak Ridge National Laboratory (ORNL) are working to change that. The team created a new aluminum alloy called RidgeAlloy that can convert low value recycled aluminum into a reliable source of material for manufacturing structural automotive parts in the United States.

Aluminum appears on DOE's critical materials list because it plays an important role in many energy technologies, including systems used to generate, transmit, store and conserve energy.

RidgeAlloy is made by remelting aluminum recovered from used products and recasting it into a new alloy designed to meet the strength, ductility and crash safety requirements of structural vehicle components. ORNL researchers developed a targeted alloy design approach that speeds up the development of new materials.

"The team advanced from a paper concept to a successful, full-scale part demonstration of a new alloy in only 15 months," said Allen Haynes, director of ORNL's Light Metals Core Program. "That's an unheard-of pace of innovation in developing complex structural alloys."

The Growing Challenge of Recycled Automotive Aluminum

Vehicles that rely heavily on aluminum began appearing in the U.S. market around 2015, including the Ford F-150 truck series, one of the first aluminum intensive models produced at large scale. Many of those vehicles are expected to reach the end of their usable life by the early 2030s. When that happens, recycling systems could receive as much as 350,000 tons of aluminum body sheet scrap every year in North America.

A large portion of this material may end up being used in lower value cast products or exported abroad. That represents a missed opportunity to reuse the metal as a domestic source of high quality aluminum.

"You can repurpose post-consumer aluminum into something non-structural like engine blocks," said Alex Plotkowski, ORNL group leader of Computational Coupled Physics. "But it won't have the properties needed for higher value, structurally sound body applications."

The main challenge comes from contamination introduced during the vehicle shredding process. Small amounts of iron from parts such as rivets and other fasteners mix into the recycled metal. These impurities make the chemical composition unpredictable and reduce performance, which prevents the material from meeting the strict standards required for structural automotive alloys.

Because of this, most lightweight vehicle parts are still made from primary aluminum produced from mined ore. That process requires significant amounts of energy.

Turning Scrap Aluminum Into a Domestic Resource

Although the United States imports most of its primary aluminum, the country has a well developed network for shredding vehicles and recovering aluminum scrap.

"Using remelted scrap instead of primary aluminum is estimated to result in up to 95% reduction in the energy needed for processing a part," said Amit Shyam, leader of ORNL's Alloy Behavior and Design Group.

To create RidgeAlloy, researchers used advanced scientific tools to design the alloy composition. High throughput computing was used to perform more than two million calculations that predicted which combinations of elements would deliver the desired mechanical properties. The team also conducted detailed materials analysis and neutron diffraction experiments at ORNL's Spallation Neutron Source, a DOE Office of Science user facility.

These experiments helped scientists understand how different impurities influence alloy performance. Neutrons are especially useful for studying metals because they can pass through dense materials without causing damage, allowing researchers to observe internal structures and changes at the atomic scale.

From Computer Models to Real Automotive Parts

After identifying the optimal alloy formula through simulations and laboratory testing, the researchers evaluated RidgeAlloy under real manufacturing conditions.

PSW Group's Trialco Aluminum in Chicago produced recycled aluminum ingots made from mixed automotive body sheet scrap that matched the RidgeAlloy design. These ingots were then sent to Falcon Lakeside Manufacturing in Michigan, where they were melted and cast into automotive components using high pressure die casting.

"The part we chose was medium-sized and moderately complex," Plotkowski said. "The ultimate goal is to eventually cast larger parts, perhaps even automotive giga-castings, but this is the first step."

Testing confirmed that RidgeAlloy contains the combination of aluminum, magnesium, silicon, iron and manganese needed for structural vehicle castings, even when the recycled metal includes higher levels of iron and silicon. The material provides the strength, corrosion resistance and ductility required for demanding applications such as vehicle underbodies, frame elements and other key structural parts.

This capability could significantly change how automotive aluminum scrap is sorted, valued and reused across North America.

Expanding the Impact Beyond the Lab

"This team figured out how to take full advantage of a national lab's world-class suite of capabilities to rapidly fill a huge gap in our understanding of lightweight automotive materials," Haynes said.

By the early 2030s, RidgeAlloy could enable recycled structural aluminum castings at volumes equal to at least half of current annual primary aluminum production in the United States. That shift could lower energy consumption, reduce manufacturing costs and strengthen domestic supply chains.

"RidgeAlloy offers the first technology capable of recapturing the value of a fast-approaching and historically massive wave of domestic, high-quality recycled automotive aluminum sheet alloys," Haynes said. "That's the big picture supply chain impact our team aimed for."

The technology may also find uses beyond passenger vehicles. Potential applications include industrial equipment, agricultural machinery, aerospace systems, mobile power generation equipment, off road vehicles such as snowmobiles and motorcycles, and marine vehicles including jet skis.

The ORNL research team included Alex Plotkowski, Amit Shyam, Allen Haynes, Sunyong Kwon, Ying Yang, Sumit Bahl, Nick Richter, Severine Cambier, Alice Perrin and Gerry Knapp. The project was supported by DOE's Office of Energy Efficiency and Renewable Energy, Vehicle Technologies Office Lightweight Metals Core Program.
articles.reasoning: The article describes a significant scientific breakthrough in recycling contaminated automotive aluminum scrap into a high-performance alloy suitable for structural vehicle parts. This innovation has broad implications for reducing energy consumption by up to 95%, lowering reliance on imported primary aluminum, and strengthening domestic supply chains. The technology addresses a large-scale environmental and industrial challenge with clear, detailed context and potential for widespread positive impact in automotive manufacturing and beyond.
articles.personality_title: Scientists create strong car parts from recycled scrap aluminum
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Many cars made since 2015 use aluminum for their body parts. When these cars are scrapped, tons of aluminum become available for recycling. But this aluminum often contains impurities that make it too weak for important car parts.

**What happened** – Scientists at Oak Ridge National Laboratory developed a new metal called RidgeAlloy. This alloy can turn mixed, contaminated aluminum scrap from old cars into strong, safe parts for vehicles. They used advanced computer models and special tests with neutrons to design and check the metal. Companies then made real car parts from this recycled aluminum to prove it works.

**Impact** – RidgeAlloy lets recycled aluminum meet the tough strength and safety rules for car frames and other key parts. This means less need to mine new aluminum, which uses a lot of energy. Using recycled aluminum can cut energy use by up to 95%. It also helps the U.S. rely less on imported aluminum and uses scrap that was hard to reuse before.

**What's next step** – Scientists plan to make bigger and more complex car parts with RidgeAlloy. The technology could expand beyond cars to machines like tractors, airplanes, and boats. By the early 2030s, RidgeAlloy could replace half of the new aluminum used in the U.S. car industry.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Oak Ridge scientists turned contaminated car scrap aluminum into a strong, recycled metal that could save energy and support making safer, more sustainable vehicles.
articles.personality_title_fr: Des scientifiques transforment la ferraille d’aluminium de voiture en métal performant pour de nouvelles voitures
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – De nombreuses voitures fabriquées depuis 2015 utilisent de l’aluminium pour leurs carrosseries. Quand ces voitures sont mises au rebut, des tonnes d’aluminium peuvent être recyclées. Mais cet aluminium contient souvent des impuretés qui le rendent trop faible pour des pièces importantes.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Des scientifiques du Laboratoire national d’Oak Ridge ont développé un nouvel alliage appelé RidgeAlloy. Cet alliage peut transformer la ferraille d’aluminium contaminée en pièces solides et sûres pour les véhicules. Ils ont utilisé des modèles informatiques avancés et des tests spéciaux aux neutrons pour concevoir et vérifier ce métal. Des entreprises ont ensuite fabriqué de vraies pièces de voiture avec cet aluminium recyclé pour prouver que cela fonctionne.

**Impact** – RidgeAlloy permet à l’aluminium recyclé de répondre aux règles strictes de résistance et de sécurité pour les cadres et autres pièces clés des voitures. Cela réduit le besoin d’extraire de l’aluminium neuf, qui consomme beaucoup d’énergie. Utiliser de l’aluminium recyclé peut diminuer la consommation d’énergie jusqu’à 95 %. Cela aide aussi les États-Unis à dépendre moins des importations d’aluminium et à valoriser une ferraille difficile à recycler auparavant.

**Prochaine étape** – Les scientifiques veulent fabriquer des pièces plus grandes et plus complexes avec RidgeAlloy. La technologie pourrait s’étendre à d’autres machines comme les tracteurs, les avions et les bateaux. D’ici le début des années 2030, RidgeAlloy pourrait remplacer la moitié de l’aluminium neuf utilisé dans l’industrie automobile américaine.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Des chercheurs d’Oak Ridge ont transformé la ferraille d’aluminium contaminée en un métal recyclé solide qui pourrait économiser de l’énergie et aider à fabriquer des véhicules plus sûrs et durables.
articles.personality_title_es: Científicos crean piezas fuertes para autos con aluminio reciclado de chatarra
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Muchos autos fabricados desde 2015 usan aluminio en sus carrocerías. Cuando estos autos se desechan, toneladas de aluminio se pueden reciclar. Pero este aluminio a menudo contiene impurezas que lo hacen débil para partes importantes.

**Qué pasó** – Científicos del Laboratorio Nacional Oak Ridge desarrollaron una nueva aleación llamada RidgeAlloy. Esta aleación puede convertir chatarra de aluminio contaminada en piezas fuertes y seguras para vehículos. Usaron modelos computarizados avanzados y pruebas especiales con neutrones para diseñar y verificar el metal. Luego, empresas fabricaron partes reales de autos con este aluminio reciclado para demostrar que funciona.

**Impacto** – RidgeAlloy permite que el aluminio reciclado cumpla con las estrictas normas de resistencia y seguridad para marcos y otras piezas clave de autos. Esto significa menos necesidad de extraer aluminio nuevo, que consume mucha energía. Usar aluminio reciclado puede reducir el consumo de energía hasta en un 95%. También ayuda a que EE. UU. dependa menos de las importaciones de aluminio y aproveche chatarra que antes era difícil de reutilizar.

**Próximo paso** – Los científicos planean fabricar partes de autos más grandes y complejas con RidgeAlloy. La tecnología podría usarse en máquinas como tractores, aviones y barcos. Para principios de la década de 2030, RidgeAlloy podría reemplazar la mitad del aluminio nuevo usado en la industria automotriz de EE. UU.

**Resumen en una frase** – Científicos de Oak Ridge convirtieron la chatarra contaminada de aluminio en un metal reciclado fuerte que podría ahorrar energía y ayudar a fabricar vehículos más seguros y sostenibles.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm-toned painting of glowing molten aluminum being poured from a futuristic crucible shaped like a stylized car body into a mold that subtly resembles an automotive frame, surrounded by abstract representations of scientific tools like neutron beams and computer data streams weaving gently through the scene, all rendered with natural, simple colors and soft lighting to evoke innovation, recycling, and sustainable metal transformation.

articles.title: China’s Great Green Wall turns Taklamakan desert into a growing carbon sink
articles.summary:
BY THE OPTIMIST DAILY EDITORIAL TEAM For decades, the Taklamakan Desert was described in stark terms: a “biological void,” a vast expanse of shifting sand where little could survive. Slightly larger than the state of Montana and ringed by mountains that block most incoming moisture, it remains one of the largest and driest deserts on […]
The post China’s Great Green Wall turns Taklamakan desert into a growing carbon sink first appeared on The Optimist Daily: Making Solutions the News.
articles.content:
BY THE OPTIMIST DAILY EDITORIAL TEAM

For decades, the Taklamakan Desert was described in stark terms: a “biological void,” a vast expanse of shifting sand where little could survive. Slightly larger than the state of Montana and ringed by mountains that block most incoming moisture, it remains one of the largest and driest deserts on Earth. Now, new research suggests that the story at its edges is changing.

After decades of large-scale tree planting, scientists report that vegetation surrounding the Taklamakan is absorbing more carbon dioxide than the desert emits, effectively turning parts of this once-expanding landscape into a carbon sink.

From desert expansion to ecological engineering

More than 95 percent of the Taklamakan is covered in moving sand. Since the 1950s, rapid urbanization and farmland expansion in China have contributed to land degradation and desertification, creating conditions that intensified sandstorms and allowed the desert to expand.

In 1978, China launched the Three-North Shelterbelt Program, widely known as the Great Green Wall. The ambitious initiative aims to plant billions of trees along the margins of the Taklamakan and Gobi deserts by 2050, creating a vast belt of vegetation designed to stabilize soil and slow desert spread.

To date, more than 66 billion trees have been planted across northern China. In 2024, officials completed the encirclement of the Taklamakan with vegetation. Forest cover across China has increased from about 10 percent of the country’s land area in 1949 to more than 25 percent today.

The project’s effectiveness in reducing sandstorms remains debated. But its impact on carbon dynamics is now coming into sharper focus.

A measurable shift in carbon balance

In a study published January 19 in Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences, researchers analyzed 25 years of ground observations and satellite data tracking precipitation, vegetation cover, photosynthesis, and carbon dioxide fluxes around the Taklamakan.

They also incorporated modeling data from the National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration’s Carbon Tracker, which maps global carbon sources and sinks.

Their findings reveal a consistent expansion of vegetation and increasing carbon uptake along the desert’s perimeter, trends that align in both time and location with the growth of the Great Green Wall.

“We found, for the first time, that human-led intervention can effectively enhance carbon sequestration in even the most extreme arid landscapes, demonstrating the potential to transform a desert into a carbon sink and halt desertification,” said study co-author Yuk Yung, a professor of planetary science at Caltech and senior research scientist at NASA’s Jet Propulsion Laboratory.

During the desert’s wet season, which runs from July to September, average monthly precipitation reached about 0.6 inches (16 millimeters), which is roughly two and a half times higher than in the dry season. That modest increase in rainfall had measurable effects.

Vegetation cover and photosynthesis along the desert’s rim increased during wetter months, lowering atmospheric carbon dioxide concentrations over the region from 416 parts per million in the dry season to 413 parts per million in the wet season.

While a three-part-per-million shift may sound small, at scale it signals a net uptake of carbon tied to living plant systems rather than unstable sand deposits.

Why this matters for long-term climate stability

Previous research suggested that the Taklamakan might act as a carbon sink because desert sands can absorb carbon dioxide. However, that storage mechanism is vulnerable. Rising temperatures can cause air trapped in sand to expand and release additional carbon dioxide, making it an unreliable long-term solution. The new findings highlight something different: carbon storage rooted in vegetation.

“Based on the results of this study, the Taklamakan Desert, although only around its rim, represents the first successful model demonstrating the possibility of transforming a desert into a carbon sink,” Yung said.

The emphasis on “around its rim” is important. The vast interior of the desert remains largely unchanged. But the vegetated perimeter is now playing an active role in carbon sequestration.

A model for other arid regions?

Questions remain about whether the Great Green Wall will significantly curb desertification or sandstorm frequency over the long term. Large-scale ecological engineering carries trade-offs, and scientists continue to assess water use, species selection, and ecosystem resilience. Still, the carbon findings offer a new lens on the project’s value.

Yung noted that the Taklamakan’s transformation “may serve as a valuable model for other desert regions.” If carefully designed and adapted to local conditions, similar interventions could help stabilize vulnerable landscapes while drawing carbon from the atmosphere.

Turning a desert into a forest is not realistic nor necessarily desirable across entire arid regions. But reinforcing the margins, stabilizing dunes, and expanding vegetation buffers may provide a pragmatic pathway for climate mitigation in some of the planet’s harshest environments.

The Taklamakan remains an immense desert. Yet along its borders, trees are reshaping the carbon equation, offering evidence that even extreme landscapes can respond to sustained, human-led restoration efforts.

Source study: Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences— Human-induced biospheric carbon sink: Impact from the Taklamakan Afforestation Project

Did this solution stand out? Share it with a friend or support our mission by becoming an Emissary.
articles.source: The Optimist Daily: Making Solutions the News
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant and measurable positive environmental impact: China's Great Green Wall project has transformed the edges of the Taklamakan Desert from a carbon source into a growing carbon sink through large-scale tree planting. This represents a meaningful advancement in climate action and desertification mitigation with broad implications for global carbon sequestration efforts. The article provides detailed context on the project, its scale, scientific findings, and potential as a model for other arid regions, fulfilling criteria for good news.
articles.personality_title: China’s Great Green Wall turns Taklamakan desert edges into a carbon sink
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – The Taklamakan Desert in China is one of the largest and driest deserts on Earth. For many years, it was known as a place where little could grow, with shifting sand covering most of its area. Since the 1950s, the desert has been expanding due to human activities and natural dryness.

**What happened** – China started a massive tree-planting project called the Great Green Wall in 1978. The goal was to plant billions of trees along the edges of deserts like the Taklamakan to stop the sand from spreading. By 2024, trees and plants surrounded the desert, increasing forest coverage in northern China from 10% to over 25%. New research shows that this vegetation is now absorbing more carbon dioxide than the desert releases, turning parts of the desert’s edge into a carbon sink.

**Impact** – This is the first clear proof that planting trees in very dry, harsh places can help remove carbon dioxide from the air. During wetter months, plants around the desert’s rim grow more and pull carbon dioxide down, helping to lower greenhouse gases. This change is important because it shows we can slow desert expansion and improve climate conditions by restoring nature, even in tough environments.

**What’s next step** – Scientists will continue studying how well this project controls desert growth and sandstorms. They will also watch how water use and plant choices affect the environment. The success around the Taklamakan could inspire similar efforts in other deserts worldwide, adapted to local conditions, to help fight climate change.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Large-scale tree planting around the Taklamakan Desert has turned its edges into a growing carbon sink, showing that restoring dry lands can help capture carbon and slow desertification.
articles.personality_title_fr: La Grande Muraille Verte de Chine transforme les bords du désert du Taklamakan en puits de carbone
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Le désert du Taklamakan en Chine est l’un des plus grands et secs déserts du monde. Pendant longtemps, il a été considéré comme un endroit où peu de vie pouvait exister, avec du sable mouvant couvrant la plupart de sa surface. Depuis les années 1950, ce désert s’est étendu à cause des activités humaines et de la sécheresse naturelle.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – La Chine a lancé en 1978 un immense projet de plantation d’arbres appelé la Grande Muraille Verte. Le but était de planter des milliards d’arbres autour des déserts comme le Taklamakan pour arrêter l’avancée du sable. En 2024, la végétation entourait le désert, faisant passer la couverture forestière dans le nord de la Chine de 10 % à plus de 25 %. Une nouvelle recherche montre que cette végétation absorbe désormais plus de dioxyde de carbone que le désert n’en émet, transformant certaines parties des bords du désert en puits de carbone.

**Impact** – C’est la première preuve claire que planter des arbres dans des endroits très secs et difficiles peut aider à retirer le dioxyde de carbone de l’air. Pendant les mois plus humides, les plantes autour du désert poussent davantage et absorbent du dioxyde de carbone, contribuant à réduire les gaz à effet de serre. Ce changement est important car il montre qu’on peut ralentir l’expansion des déserts et améliorer le climat en restaurant la nature, même dans des environnements difficiles.

**Prochaine étape** – Les scientifiques continueront à étudier l’efficacité du projet pour contrôler la progression du désert et les tempêtes de sable. Ils surveilleront aussi l’impact de l’utilisation de l’eau et des choix de plantes sur l’environnement. Le succès autour du Taklamakan pourrait inspirer des efforts similaires dans d’autres déserts du monde, adaptés aux conditions locales, pour aider à lutter contre le changement climatique.

**Conclusion en une phrase** – La plantation massive d’arbres autour du désert du Taklamakan a transformé ses bords en un puits de carbone croissant, montrant que restaurer les terres arides peut aider à capter le carbone et ralentir la désertification.
articles.personality_title_es: La Gran Muralla Verde de China convierte los bordes del desierto de Taklamakan en un sumidero de carbono
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – El desierto de Taklamakan en China es uno de los desiertos más grandes y secos del mundo. Durante mucho tiempo, se consideró un lugar donde casi nada podía crecer, con arena movediza cubriendo la mayor parte del área. Desde la década de 1950, el desierto ha ido creciendo debido a actividades humanas y la sequía natural.

**Qué pasó** – China inició en 1978 un gran proyecto de plantación de árboles llamado la Gran Muralla Verde. El objetivo era plantar miles de millones de árboles en los bordes de desiertos como el Taklamakan para detener la expansión de la arena. En 2024, la vegetación rodeaba el desierto, aumentando la cobertura forestal en el norte de China del 10 % a más del 25 %. Una nueva investigación muestra que esta vegetación ahora absorbe más dióxido de carbono del que el desierto emite, convirtiendo partes de los bordes en un sumidero de carbono.

**Impacto** – Esta es la primera prueba clara de que plantar árboles en lugares muy secos y difíciles puede ayudar a sacar dióxido de carbono del aire. Durante los meses más húmedos, las plantas alrededor del desierto crecen más y reducen el dióxido de carbono en el aire, ayudando a bajar los gases que causan el calentamiento global. Este cambio es importante porque muestra que podemos frenar la expansión del desierto y mejorar el clima restaurando la naturaleza, incluso en lugares difíciles.

**Próximo paso** – Los científicos seguirán estudiando qué tan bien el proyecto controla el crecimiento del desierto y las tormentas de arena. También observarán cómo el uso del agua y la elección de plantas afectan al ambiente. El éxito en el Taklamakan podría inspirar esfuerzos similares en otros desiertos del mundo, adaptados a las condiciones locales, para ayudar a combatir el cambio climático.

**Resumen en una frase** – La plantación masiva de árboles alrededor del desierto de Taklamakan ha convertido sus bordes en un sumidero de carbono creciente, mostrando que restaurar tierras áridas puede ayudar a capturar carbono y frenar la desertificación.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm painting of the vast Taklamakan Desert’s sandy interior surrounded by a vibrant, thriving green belt of young trees and shrubs encircling its edges, with subtle swirling sand dunes meeting the lush vegetation, under a soft sky showing gentle seasonal rain clouds nourishing the plants, symbolizing the transformation of harsh desert margins into a growing carbon sink through sustained ecological restoration.

articles.title: A Most Unusual Lake
articles.summary: Lake Unter-See in Antarctica, sealed beneath thick ice, contains unusually high levels of dissolved oxygen and cone-shaped microbial reefs resembling some of Earth’s oldest fossils.
articles.content:
February 16, 2026

Scientists estimate that Earth is home to more than 100 million lakes. Among the most unusual is Lake Unter-See, one of Antarctica’s largest and deepest surface lakes, known for its distinctive water chemistry. Its ice-covered waters have exceptionally high levels of dissolved oxygen, low dissolved carbon dioxide, and a strongly alkaline (basic) pH.

The OLI (Operational Land Imager) on Landsat 9 captured this image on February 16, 2026, during the Antarctic summer. Most of the lake's water comes from seasonal meltwater draining from the margins of the nearby Anuchin Glacier, which flows south from the Gruber Mountains in Queen Maud Land.

With mean annual temperatures of about minus 10 degrees Celsius (14 degrees Fahrenheit), Lake Unter-See remains frozen year-round, its waters sealed beneath several meters of ice. Sunlight penetrates the ice and warms the water below, but the cold surface and strong winds drive evaporation and sublimation, preventing significant surface melting. The lake's maximum depth is thought to reach nearly 170 meters (558 feet).

The lake’s water chemistry is unusual partly because it is one of the only perennially frozen lakes with a community of large, conical stromatolites. The layered microbial reef structures grow slowly upward as photosynthetic microbes—primarily cyanobacteria—trap sediment on their sticky surfaces and form calcium carbonate mineral crusts. These conical stromatolites—as well as pinnacle and flat forms of the microbial communities—release oxygen that becomes trapped under the ice, increasing its concentration in the lake.

Lake Unter-See's stromatolites, discovered by SETI geobiologist Dale Andersen and colleagues in 2011, offer a glimpse into a time more than 3 billion years ago, when microbes were the only form of life on Earth. The formations are thought to be modern, living examples of the organisms that likely produced some of Earth's oldest fossils—stromatolites found in places such as southwestern Greenland and western Australia.

The scientists noted that similar periodic flooding may provide "biological stimuli to other carbon dioxide-depleted Antarctic ecosystems and perhaps even icy lakes on early Mars.”

Some Antarctic lakes, such as Lake Joyce in the McMurdo Dry Valleys, contain conical stromatolites, but they reach only a few centimeters tall. By contrast, the formations in Lake Unter-See tower up to half a meter. Scientists think Unter-See's stromatolites grow unusually tall because they are sheltered from tides and waves beneath permanent ice, live in exceptionally clear waters with little sediment, grow toward limited light, and face little grazing. The lake’s largest creatures are tardigrades—microscopic "water bear" invertebrates known for their ability to survive in extreme environments.

Astrobiologists also point to the lake as a possible analog for the type of environment where life might have formed or survived on icy moons with oceans such as Europa and Enceladus, or perhaps on Mars, which has ice caps and glaciers.

Yet despite its seemingly stable conditions, Lake Unter-See occasionally experiences abrupt changes. During fieldwork in 2019, researchers observed an increase in the lake’s water levels. The team, led by scientists at the University of Ottawa, later analyzed elevation data from NASA’s ICESat-2 (Ice, Cloud, and Land Elevation Satellite-2) and confirmed a 2-meter rise was caused by a glacial lake outburst flood from nearby Lake Ober-See.

The University of Ottawa team also showed that the outburst flood had released 17.5 million cubic meters of meltwater, altering Unter-See’s pH and replenishing it with carbon dioxide-rich waters that likely enhanced the productivity of the lake's microbial life. The scientists noted that similar periodic flooding may provide "biological stimuli to other carbon dioxide-depleted Antarctic ecosystems and perhaps even icy lakes on early Mars.”

NASA Earth Observatory image by Michala Garrison, using Landsat data from the U.S. Geological Survey. Story by Adam Voiland.

Downloads February 16, 2026

References & Resources
articles.reasoning: The article describes the discovery and study of Lake Unter-See, an unusual Antarctic lake with unique microbial life that offers insights into early Earth conditions and potential extraterrestrial life habitats. This represents a significant scientific breakthrough with broad implications for understanding life on Earth and possibly other planets, thus having meaningful positive impact and inspiring curiosity.
articles.personality_title: Lake Unter-See reveals ancient microbial life beneath Antarctic ice
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Lake Unter-See is a large, deep lake in Antarctica covered by thick ice all year. It is unusual because of its special water chemistry, with very high oxygen levels and low carbon dioxide, and it hosts unique microbial life.

**What happened** – Scientists studied Lake Unter-See using satellite images and fieldwork. They found cone-shaped microbial reefs called stromatolites growing under the ice. These stromatolites are similar to some of the oldest fossils on Earth and produce oxygen that stays trapped in the lake’s water. In 2019, a flood from a nearby lake raised Unter-See’s water level and added carbon dioxide, helping the microbes grow.

**Impact** – This lake is special because its stromatolites grow much taller than those in other Antarctic lakes, thanks to clear water and protection under ice. Studying these microbes helps scientists understand early life on Earth, over 3 billion years ago. It also gives clues about how life might exist on icy worlds like Jupiter’s moon Europa or Mars.

**What's next step** – Researchers will keep monitoring the lake to see how changes like flooding affect its ecosystem. They also hope to learn more about how life survives in extreme cold and low carbon dioxide, which can help future studies of planets and moons beyond Earth.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Lake Unter-See’s icy waters hold living microbial reefs that connect us to ancient Earth and hint at possible life on other icy worlds.
articles.personality_title_fr: Le lac Unter-See révèle une vie microbienne ancienne sous la glace de l’Antarctique
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Le lac Unter-See est un grand lac profond en Antarctique, couvert toute l’année par une épaisse couche de glace. Il est unique en raison de sa chimie de l’eau particulière, avec des niveaux très élevés d’oxygène et peu de dioxyde de carbone, et abrite une vie microbienne spéciale.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Des scientifiques ont étudié le lac Unter-See avec des images satellites et des travaux sur le terrain. Ils ont découvert des récifs microbiens en forme de cônes, appelés stromatolites, qui poussent sous la glace. Ces stromatolites ressemblent à certains des plus anciens fossiles sur Terre et produisent de l’oxygène qui reste piégé dans l’eau du lac. En 2019, une inondation d’un lac voisin a fait monter le niveau d’eau d’Unter-See et apporté du dioxyde de carbone, aidant la croissance des microbes.

**Impact** – Ce lac est spécial car ses stromatolites poussent beaucoup plus haut que dans d’autres lacs antarctiques, grâce à une eau claire et à la protection de la glace. Étudier ces microbes aide les scientifiques à comprendre la vie ancienne sur Terre, il y a plus de 3 milliards d’années. Cela donne aussi des indices sur la vie possible sur des mondes glacés comme Europe, lune de Jupiter, ou Mars.

**Prochaine étape** – Les chercheurs continueront de surveiller le lac pour voir comment des changements comme les inondations affectent cet écosystème. Ils veulent aussi mieux comprendre comment la vie survit dans des conditions extrêmes, ce qui pourra aider les futures recherches sur d’autres planètes et lunes.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Les eaux glacées du lac Unter-See abritent des récifs microbiens vivants qui nous relient à la Terre ancienne et suggèrent la possibilité de vie sur d’autres mondes glacés.
articles.personality_title_es: El lago Unter-See revela vida microbiana antigua bajo el hielo antártico
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – El lago Unter-See es un lago grande y profundo en la Antártida, cubierto por un grueso hielo todo el año. Es inusual por su química especial del agua, con niveles muy altos de oxígeno y poco dióxido de carbono, y alberga una vida microbiana única.

**Qué pasó** – Científicos estudiaron el lago Unter-See con imágenes satelitales y trabajo de campo. Encontraron arrecifes microbianos en forma de conos llamados estromatolitos creciendo bajo el hielo. Estos estromatolitos son similares a algunos de los fósiles más antiguos de la Tierra y producen oxígeno que queda atrapado en el agua del lago. En 2019, una inundación de un lago cercano elevó el nivel del agua y añadió dióxido de carbono, ayudando a los microbios a crecer.

**Impacto** – Este lago es especial porque sus estromatolitos crecen mucho más altos que en otros lagos antárticos, gracias al agua clara y la protección bajo el hielo. Estudiar estos microbios ayuda a entender la vida temprana en la Tierra, hace más de 3 mil millones de años. También da pistas sobre cómo podría existir vida en mundos helados como Europa, luna de Júpiter, o Marte.

**Próximo paso** – Los investigadores seguirán monitoreando el lago para ver cómo cambios como las inundaciones afectan su ecosistema. También quieren aprender más sobre cómo la vida sobrevive en condiciones extremas, lo que puede ayudar a futuros estudios de planetas y lunas fuera de la Tierra.

**Resumen en una frase** – Las aguas heladas del lago Unter-See contienen arrecifes microbianos vivos que nos conectan con la Tierra antigua y sugieren la posible vida en otros mundos helados.
articles.image_prompt: A serene, softly lit Antarctic lake scene beneath a thick translucent ice sheet, revealing towering, cone-shaped stromatolite formations rising from crystal-clear alkaline waters, with delicate cyanobacteria textures and tiny resilient tardigrade silhouettes subtly nestled among the microbial reefs, framed by distant icy glaciers and gentle sunlight filtering through a pale sky.

articles.title: The feds pulled $1.5B from tribal clean energy. Tribes are finding another way.
articles.content:
Across tribal nations, hosting a convening with dinner and a tour of an ambitious new project is a familiar scene. But for David Harper, a member of the Colorado River Indian Tribes and CEO of the newly created tribal energy financing organization Huurav, a recent gathering felt different. Last week, at the Bluewater Resort and Casino on the Colorado River Indian Tribes reservation in western Arizona, Huurav met with tribal leaders, investors, and farmers to kick off the tribe’s first agrivoltaics project: a practice that allows for growing crops beneath solar panels.

The project marks a significant breakthrough for the tribe and the broader tribal clean energy landscape, arriving on the heels of a devastating blow to federal support. In October of last year, the passage of President Donald Trump’s tax bill, colloquially known as the “One Big Beautiful Bill Act,” stripped roughly $1.5 billion in federal funding earmarked for tribal renewable energy and climate resilience projects.

“Were we surprised by the claw back? No, they’ve done it before,” said Huurav’s Harper. “Have they reneged on their promise of our treaties? Yes, of course. So does that immobilize us and not be able to survive? No, what it does is it helps us, it makes us create a better pathway for ourselves.”

With nearly 1,600 projects by tribal governments and Native entities losing some or all of their federal funding, tribes have been forced to get creative. To keep clean energy projects alive, tribes are turning to philanthropy, low-interest loans, and nonprofits to bridge the massive financial gap.

To support our nonprofit environmental journalism, please consider disabling your ad-blocker to allow ads on Grist. Here's How

The Biden-era Inflation Reduction Act of 2022 represented a historic investment in tribes and renewables, but experts and tribal leaders felt it was still insufficient to address historic inequities in Indian Country. “Given the role that the federal government itself played in generating these investment needs through things like land theft, disinvestment, [and] cultural destruction in Indian Country, a one-time infusion of cash isn’t enough,” said Robert Maxim, a member of the Mashpee Wampanoag Tribe and Brookings fellow who recently co-authored a report on the One Big Beautiful Bill Act’s impact. Maxim emphasized that federal funding for Indian Country is a fundamental part of trust and treaty obligations.

“Things like a clean environment, adequate energy to supply homes, basic investments in electricity, and the ability to do all that without higher levels of pollution and environmental degradation than the U.S population as a whole are all key to that trust and treaty relationship,” said Maxim.

To fill the void, nonprofits like the Alliance for Tribal Clean Energy are stepping in. Through its Indigenous Power and Light Fund, backed by philanthropic partners like the MacArthur Foundation, the Alliance serves as a critical lifeline. Huurav is taking a lead role as well, drawing on its expertise gained through its participation in the inaugural cohort of the Agrivoltaics Growth through Resilience and Innovation program, run by the National Laboratory of the Rockies and the Farmland Trust, a conservation-focused agriculture nonprofit.

While commercial lenders are difficult to find across tribal nations, community financial development institutions, known as CFDIs, are meant to bridge that gap by offering funding to Native-led organizations and tribes seeking to invest in renewables on tribal land.

To support our nonprofit environmental journalism, please consider disabling your ad-blocker to allow ads on Grist. Here's How

A recent survey found that more than half of Native-operated CFDIs, financial institutions that provide credit to underserved populations, cite lack of funding as one of their biggest challenges, and that financial barrier compounds existing energy inequalities. A 2023 Department of Energy report revealed that tribal households face an energy burden 28 percent higher than the national average. In the Southwest, for example, the Hopi Tribe and Navajo Nation have the highest rates of unelectrified homes and often rely on coal or propane for heat. Many tribes lack reliable transmission lines, leaving them vulnerable to extreme weather events, like floods and wildfires, fueled by climate change.

Despite these obstacles, Indigenous communities are pushing forward by courting sustainable investors to achieve energy independence and lower utility costs. On the Hawaiian island of Molokai, for instance, Native Hawaiians are leveraging profitable renewable energy projects to reduce exorbitant utility bills while simultaneously advancing their landback initiatives. “They’re making the case that they can get investors to invest in their landback projects,” said Kyle Whyte, Citizen Potawatomi Nation and University of Michigan professor of environmental justice. “One of the ways that they’ll be able to succeed in governing that land is through profitable renewable energy projects that would reduce people’s utility costs.”

However, some tribes have turned away from solar and wind altogether, opting instead to pursue active federal grants aligned with the Trump administration’s agenda to expand domestic energy. This includes tapping into almost $172 million in Department of Energy geothermal funding or seeking transmission upgrades backed by a $1.6 billion federal loan guarantee.

Ultimately, the push for clean energy is about self-determination and resilience. As David Harper of Huurav puts it: “We don’t trust the federal government, but we have to work with them to understand that we have to continue in our process of survival and self-sustainability.”
articles.reasoning: The article highlights Indigenous tribes overcoming significant federal funding cuts to advance clean energy projects, demonstrating resilience and innovation with broad environmental and social benefits. It focuses on a specific agrivoltaics project and the broader movement toward tribal energy independence and sustainability, addressing historic inequities and energy burdens in tribal communities.
articles.personality_title: Tribal nations advance clean energy despite federal funding cuts
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Tribal communities in the United States have faced a recent loss of $1.5 billion in federal funding for renewable energy projects due to changes in tax laws. This funding was meant to support clean energy and climate resilience for tribal nations.

**What happened** – In response, tribes are finding new ways to keep their clean energy plans alive. One example is the Colorado River Indian Tribes, which recently launched their first agrivoltaics project. This project grows crops beneath solar panels, combining farming with solar energy. Tribal leaders, investors, and farmers gathered at the Bluewater Resort and Casino in Arizona to start this work. Organizations like Huurav, a tribal energy financing group, and nonprofits such as the Alliance for Tribal Clean Energy are helping bridge the gap left by federal cuts.

**Impact** – This effort is important because many tribal areas suffer from higher energy costs and less reliable electricity than other parts of the U.S. For example, some tribes rely on coal or propane for heat and have homes without electricity. By developing their own renewable energy projects, tribes can reduce these energy burdens, save money, and build more control over their land and resources. These projects also honor treaty promises and support tribal self-sustainability.

**What’s next step** – Tribes will continue to seek partnerships with investors, nonprofits, and community financial institutions to fund more clean energy projects. Some tribes are exploring federal grants for geothermal energy or transmission upgrades. The goal is to grow energy independence, improve living conditions, and support economic development on tribal lands.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Despite losing federal funding, tribal nations are creating innovative clean energy projects to improve their communities’ energy access and independence.
articles.personality_title_fr: Les nations tribales avancent dans l’énergie propre malgré les coupes fédérales
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les communautés tribales des États-Unis ont récemment perdu 1,5 milliard de dollars de financement fédéral pour des projets d’énergie renouvelable à cause de changements dans la loi fiscale. Ce financement devait soutenir l’énergie propre et la résilience climatique pour ces nations.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – En réponse, les tribus trouvent de nouvelles façons de maintenir leurs projets d’énergie propre. Par exemple, les tribus indiennes de la rivière Colorado ont lancé leur premier projet d’agrivoltaïsme, qui consiste à cultiver des plantes sous des panneaux solaires. Des dirigeants tribaux, des investisseurs et des agriculteurs se sont réunis en Arizona pour débuter ce projet. Des organisations comme Huurav, un groupe de financement énergétique tribal, et des associations comme l’Alliance pour l’énergie propre tribale aident à combler le manque de fonds.

**Impact** – Cette initiative est importante car de nombreuses zones tribales ont des coûts d’énergie élevés et un accès moins fiable à l’électricité que le reste des États-Unis. Certaines tribus dépendent encore du charbon ou du propane pour se chauffer, et beaucoup ont des maisons sans électricité. En développant leurs propres projets d’énergie renouvelable, les tribus peuvent réduire ces difficultés, économiser de l’argent et mieux gérer leurs terres et ressources. Ces projets respectent aussi les promesses des traités et soutiennent l’autosuffisance tribale.

**Étape suivante** – Les tribus continueront à chercher des partenariats avec des investisseurs, des associations et des institutions financières communautaires pour financer plus de projets d’énergie propre. Certaines explorent aussi des subventions fédérales pour la géothermie ou la modernisation des réseaux électriques. L’objectif est de renforcer leur indépendance énergétique, améliorer les conditions de vie et soutenir le développement économique sur leurs terres.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Malgré la perte de financement fédéral, les nations tribales créent des projets innovants d’énergie propre pour améliorer l’accès à l’énergie et leur autonomie.
articles.personality_title_es: Las naciones tribales avanzan en energía limpia pese a recortes federales
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Las comunidades tribales en Estados Unidos han perdido recientemente 1.500 millones de dólares en fondos federales para proyectos de energía renovable debido a cambios en la ley fiscal. Estos fondos estaban destinados a apoyar la energía limpia y la resiliencia climática para las naciones tribales.

**Qué pasó** – En respuesta, las tribus están encontrando nuevas formas de mantener sus proyectos de energía limpia. Un ejemplo es la tribu india del río Colorado, que lanzó su primer proyecto de agrivoltaica, que consiste en cultivar plantas debajo de paneles solares. Líderes tribales, inversionistas y agricultores se reunieron en Arizona para iniciar este proyecto. Organizaciones como Huurav, un grupo de financiamiento energético tribal, y organizaciones sin fines de lucro como la Alianza por la Energía Limpia Tribal están ayudando a cubrir el vacío dejado por los recortes federales.

**Impacto** – Este esfuerzo es importante porque muchas áreas tribales enfrentan costos de energía más altos y un acceso menos confiable a la electricidad que otras partes de EE.UU. Algunas tribus dependen del carbón o propano para calefacción y tienen hogares sin electricidad. Al desarrollar sus propios proyectos de energía renovable, las tribus pueden reducir estas cargas energéticas, ahorrar dinero y tener más control sobre sus tierras y recursos. Estos proyectos también honran los tratados y apoyan la autosuficiencia tribal.

**Próximo paso** – Las tribus seguirán buscando asociaciones con inversionistas, organizaciones sin fines de lucro e instituciones financieras comunitarias para financiar más proyectos de energía limpia. Algunas tribus exploran subvenciones federales para energía geotérmica o mejoras en la transmisión eléctrica. El objetivo es aumentar la independencia energética, mejorar las condiciones de vida y apoyar el desarrollo económico en tierras tribales.

**Resumen en una frase** – A pesar de perder fondos federales, las naciones tribales están creando proyectos innovadores de energía limpia para mejorar el acceso y la independencia energética de sus comunidades.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of a vibrant tribal farmland where rows of solar panels arch gently over flourishing crops, with stylized river waters flowing nearby symbolizing the Colorado River Indian Tribes, under a soft sky blending natural earth tones and muted blues, evoking resilience and self-sustaining clean energy rooted in Indigenous land and community.
